Facts.text: Leo I crowned Eastern Roman Emperor, 1st to be crowned by the Patriarch of Constantinople
Battle of Montesarchio: Prince of Benevento, Pandulf IV killed battling the encroaching Normans in southern Italy
Mongolian forces led by General Batu capture and burn the important Russian city of Vladimir, after an eight-day siege
Treaty of Brussels: Habsburgers split into Spanish and Austrian Branches
Michail Romanov (aged 16) becomes Tsar of Russia
Academie Française begins Dictionary of French Language
Thomas Siddel is reported to have harvested a 17-1/4 lb (7.8 kg) 47-ibch long potato from his garden in Chester, England
Austria and Prussia sign an anti-French covenant
Domenico Cimarosa's opera "Il Matrimonio Segreto" premieres in Vienna
11th Amendment to US Constitution affirming power of state courts is ratified
Dutch Prince William V accepts British occupation of Dutch Indies
8.2 earthquake shakes New Madrid, Missouri
Baltimore becomes the 1st American city lit by gas street lamps with the first turned on at Market and Lemon Streets (currently Baltimore and Holliday Streets)
1st successful US educational magazine "Academician" begins (NYC)
Ballet (Deserter) introduced to US at Bowery Theater (NYC)
Belgium adopts its Constitution
Battle of Debre Tabor: Ras Ali Alula, Regent of the Emperor of Ethiopia, defeats warlord Wube Haile Maryam of Semien
Colonial Tasmanian Parliament passes the first piece of legislation, the Electoral Act of 1856, anywhere in the world providing for elections by way of a secret ballot
Federal fleet attack on Roanoke Island, North Carolina
HMS Orpheus sinks off the coast of Auckland, New Zealand, killing 189
Federal troops occupy Jacksonville, Florida
Alcorn Agricultural and Mechanical College opens by Lorman, Mississippi - 1st US black land grant college
US President Grant's private secretary Orville acquitted in Whiskey Ring
First American Guernsey Cattle Club is organized in New York City
Battle at Ingogo, Transvaal: Boers defeat a superior British force
Lt-colonel Borgnis-Desbordes founds Fort Bamako, Niger
Canadian Rugby Football Union forms
Astronomical Society of Pacific holds 1st meeting in San Francisco
Vanderbilt University claims it participated in the first organised intercollegiate basketball game at the Nashville YMCA Gymnasium; Vanderbilt beats YMCA, 9-3
The Cripple Creek miners' strike, led by the Western Federation of Miners, begins in Cripple Creek, Colorado
British troops vacate Vaal Krantz, Natal
VVV '03 soccer club is established in the Dutch southeastern city of Venlo
Baltimore catches fire (1500 buildings destroyed in 80 blocks)
Dominican Republic signs treaty turning over customs collection to US
Conservative coalition take over Reichstag in Germany after rallying conservatives against the threat of a socialist government
The Mud March, first large procession organized by the National Union of Women's Suffrage Societies (NUWSS)
Edmond Rostand's play "Chantecler" premieres in Paris
2nd Dutch 11 city skate (Coen de Koenig wins (11:40)
Steel work completed on Exposition (Civic) Auditorium, San Francisco
First wireless message sent from a moving train to a station is received
Second Battle of Masurian Lakes: German armies surrounded a Russian army
Russian Imperial Navy Admiral Kolchak, leader of anti-communist "White Movement" executed by Bolshevik firing squad in Irkutsk, Russian SFSR
John Willard's play "Cat & the Canary" premieres in NYC
1st solo flight from England to Australia takes off from Croydon, piloted by Australian aviator Bert Hinkler (arrives 15 ½ days later)
American opera "Peter Ibbetson" by Deems Taylor premieres at Metropolitan Opera, New York City
Colonial troops in Suriname kill 2 demonstrators
Social-Democrat meeting in Berlin "As thousands cheer" Marxism is dead
1st contract for TVA power, Tupelo, Mississippi
Felix the Cat, animated film released by Van Beuren Studios licensed from Otto Messmer
Flag of the US Vice President authorized
First indoor 15' pole vault by Cornelius Warmerdam at 15' 3/8"
Shoe rationing begins in US (may purchase up to 3 more pairs in 1942)
Germans launch counteroffensive at Anzio, Italy
Harry Truman appoints Irwin C. Mollison judge of US Customs Court
London, Washington and Moscow discuss final phase of World War II
US 76th/5th Infantry divisions begin crossing the Sauer river near Echternach, Luxembourg, and breach the Siegfried Line
Filibuster in US Senate kills FEPC (Fair Employment Practices Committee) bill
The UK announces its intention to end the Mandate for Palestine
After winning Lake Placid (1932) and Garmisch-Partenkirchen (1936) Olympic ski jumping gold medals, Birger Ruud comes out of retirement to win silver in St. Moritz in a Norwegian medal sweep
Georges Bidault forms French government
Senator Joe McCarthy finds "communists" in US Department of State (equiv. Ministry of Foreign Affairs)
Dodgers officially become the Los Angeles Dodgers, Inc
First showing of Dutch automatic transmission car, the DAF 600

American pilots Bob Timm and John Cook break recently set airplane flight endurance record, exceeding 1550 hours (64 days, 22 hours, and 19 minutes), landing "The Hacienda", their modified Cessna 172 where they started, in Las Vegas, Nevada
Old handwriting found in at Qumran, near the Dead Sea
Gas explosion in Luisanthal coal mine Voelklingen Germany kills 298
Baskin-Robbins introduces Beatle Nut ice cream
The Beatles land at New York's JFK airport, for 1st US visit
Operations begin at Grupo Folklorico Antiyano on Curacao
US begins regular bombing & strafing of North Vietnam
WVIZ TV channel 25 in Cleveland, Ohio (PBS) begins broadcasting
KWCM TV channel 10 in Appleton, MN (PBS) begins broadcasting
A fire at a restaurant in Montgomery, Alabama kills 25 people
Belgium government of Vanden Boeynants falls
WLED TV channel 49 in Littleton, NH (PBS) begins broadcasting
British rock band The Who record their song "Pinball Wizard" at Morgan Studio's, London
Diane Crump (20) becomes 1st woman jockey to ride against men at a major US racetrack, aboard Bridle ’n Bit in a race at Hialeah Park, in Hialeah, Florida
The original Hetch Hetchy Moccasin Powerhouse is removed from service
"Hollywood Palace" last airs on ABC TV
"Jingle Jangle" hits #10 on the pop singles chart by Archies
LSU's "Pistol" Pete Marovich scores 69 pts in losing cause
Switzerland votes for national women's suffrage in a referendum - last European country to do so except for Liechtenstein
North Ireland's United Loyalist Council hold a one-day strike, loyalist paramilitaries forcibly try to stop people going to work
Rangers shut out Islanders 6-0 for the first time
US Senate creates Select Committee on Presidential Campaign Activities
Grenada gains independence from Britain (National Day)
FCC raids & shuts down pirate radio station WCPR (Brooklyn New York)
Larry Groce's "Junk Food Junkie" peaks at #9
World's then largest telescope at 605 cm, the BTA-6 begins operations in the USSR
Soyuz 24 launches with 2 cosmonauts
Ethiopian offensive in Ogaden desert
"Supertrain", TV Anthology, Superbomb of 1979, debuts on NBC
Colonel Benjedid Chadli succeeds president Boumedienne in Algeria
Faoud Bacchus scores 250 for WI v India at Kanpur
Luis A Monge elected President of Costa Rica
"José Cuervo" single released by Shelly West (Billboard Song of the Year 1983)
Elizabeth Dole is sworn in as the 1st US female secretary of transportation
American astronaut Bruce McCandless makes 1st untethered space walk
David (born without immunity system) touches his mother for 1st time at age 12
"New York, New York" becomes the official anthem of New York City
Marshall U's Bruce Morris scores a basket from 92'5"
NY Devil Don Lever becomes 57th NHLer to score 300 goals
"Ronnies Rap" by Ron & DC Crew peaks at #93
Dennis Conner and 'Stars & Stripes' return the America's Cup yachting trophy to US
Police in South Korea make hundreds of arrests during protest demonstrations, the country's biggest for 6 years, after a student died in custody
Karachi police kill 22 anti-nationalistic demonstrators
Lisa Leslie of Morningside HS in Inglewood California scores 101 in 1st half, South Torrance HS decides not to play 2nd half & loses 102-24
USSR Communist party agrees to allow opposition political parties
Jean-Bertrand Aristide sworn in as Haiti's 1st elected president
American actor and singer Jim Nabors (Gomer Pyle, USMC) undergoes a liver transplant
US and Russia announce a summit set for Helsinki on March 20-21
NHL's Dallas Stars retire Neal Broten's #7
XVIII Winter Olympic Games open in Nagano, Japan
Crown Prince Abdullah becomes the King of Jordan on the death of his father, King Hussein bin Talal
Bahria University is established through Presidential Ordinance No. V of 2000 by the Government of Pakistan
Britain's Ellen MacArthur becomes the fastest person to sail solo around the world, taking 71 days, 14 hours, 18 minutes, and 33 seconds
Bushfires in Victoria leave 173 dead in the worst natural disaster in Australian history
Azerbaijan launches its first satellite, Azerspace-1
Eugène Delacroix's painting "Liberty Leading the People" is vandalized at the Louvre-Lens museum, Northern France
German treasurer Helmut Linssen resigns amid a scandal involving offshore banking accounts in the Bahamas and Panama
XXII Winter Olympic Games open in Sochi, Russia
Super Bowl L, Levi's Stadium, Santa Clara, CA: Denver Broncos beat Carolina Panthers, 24–10; MVP: Von Miller, Denver, LB
"The Los Angeles Times" is bought by LA doctor Patrick Soon-Shiong from Tronc for $500 million
All citrus fruit can be traced to the southeastern foothills of the Himalayas, according to a DNA study published in "Nature"
Armed groups release 300 child soldiers from Yambio, South Sudan according to UN
DNA analysis of Chedder Man, UK's oldest complete skeleton shows he had dark skin and blue eyes
White House aide Rob Porter resigns in wake of physical abuse allegations by ex-wives
First reports of poisonous homemade alcohol killing people in the Indian states of Uttarakhand and Uttar Pradesh, will go on to kill about 100
France recalls its Italian ambassador from Rome after Italian deputy PM Luigi Di Maio met with yellow vest protesters
Measles cases in Europe highest in a decade, tripling in a year to 82,596 according to WHO
Measles outbreak declared in the Philippines with 1,813 cases and 26 deaths
New kangaroo fossil research published from Riversleigh, Australia, show Kangaroos learned to hop 20 million years ago, much earlier than first thought
Sierra Leone President Julius Maada Bio declares national emergency over rape and sexual assault violence after instances double
Himalayan glacier crashes into the Dhauliganga river, destroying a dam and causing a huge flood in Uttarakhand province, India, killing 26, leaving 150 missing
Sarah Thomas becomes the first woman to referee a Super Bowl in Tampa Bay, Florida
European security facing its most dangerous moment since the Cold War, amid fears of a Russian invasion of Ukraine, according to EU foreign policy chief
UK Metropolitan police officer David Carrick jailed for life for 85 serious offences including 48 rapes over 17 years, amid calls for a radical overhaul of the police service
Greek island of Santorini is placed under a state of emergency amid a swarm of earthquakes that began 27 January
Edward of Caernarfon (laterEdward II) becomes the first English Prince of Wales
Giovanni Maria del Monte elected PopeJulius III
KingPhilip IIforms Inquisition in South America
Nicolas Fouquetappointed France's Superintendent of Finances
Dutch PrinceWilliam IIIdances in premiere of "Ballet of Peace"
PoetLord Byron(6th Baron Byron) makes his maiden speech in House of Lords
"Sketches by Boz" (essays) published byCharles Dickens
Henry Claydeclares in the Senate, "I would rather be right than president"
French writerGustave Flaubertis acquitted on a charge of obscenity for his work "Madame Bovary"
Last bare knuckle championJohn L. SullivanKOs Paddy Ryan in Miss
Philadelphia Athletics' manager and ownerConnie Macksells future Baseball Hall of Fame pitcher Rube Waddell to St Louis Browns for $5,000
Charlie Chaplindebuts silent film character The Tramp in "Kid Auto Races at Venice"
Benito Mussolini's Italian government exchanges diplomats with USSR
AnthropologistRaymond Dartpublishes his landmark paper about the discovery of Australopithecus africanus (the Taung Child), confirmingCharles Darwin's prediction of an African human origin
Walt Disney's second feature length movie, "Pinocchio" premieres (NYC)
Frank Sinatra&Tommy Dorseyorchestra record "Everything Happens to Me"
Bing Crosbyrecords "Swinging on a Star" for Decca Records (Academy Award Best Original Song)
Omar BradleysucceedsDwight Eisenhoweras US Army Chief of Staff
Joe DiMaggiobecomes 1st $100,000 a year baseball player, signs new contract with the New York Yankees
United States formally recognizes the State of Vietnam as the legitimate government of Vietnam, withBảo Đạias leader
Fidel Castroproclaims new Cuban constitution
Jane Fondamade her acting debut in the NBC drama "A String of Beads"
US PresidentJFKbegins blockade of Cuba by banning all Cuban imports and exports.
Roger Sessions' 5th Symphony premieres in Philadelphia withEugene Ormandyconducting the Philadelphia Orchestra
George Harrison's tonsils removed
"This IsTom Jones" debuts on ABC TV
Al-Fatah-leaderYasser Arafatbecomes president of PLO
Dutch Olympic 1,500m and 5,000m speed skating championArd Schenkwins his 3rd gold medal at the Sapporo Winter Games when he takes out the 10,000m
Iggy PopandThe Stoogesrelease their influential 3rd album "Raw Power"
American left-wing guerilla group Symbionese Liberation Army claim responsibility for the kidnapping ofPatty Hearst, daughter of American publishing heir Randolph Hearst
Mel Brooks' western spoof film "Blazing Saddles," starring Cleavon Little andGene Wilder, premieres at the Pickwick Drive-In in Burbank, California; patrons watch from horseback rather than cars
UK Prime MinisterEdward Heathcalls for a 'snap' general election in the midst of a protracted miners' strike
Toronto centerDarryl Sittlersets NHL record with 10 points in a Maple Leafs' 11-4 win over Boston at Maple Leaf Gardens; 6 goals, 4 assists
Pink Floydpremiere their live version of "The Wall" at the Los Angeles Memorial Sports Arena in Los Angeles, California
Michael Jacksonawarded a 4-ft-high platinum disc by CBS
Miguel Ángel Félix Gallardoorders the kidnapping of DEA agent Enrique "Kiki" Camarena in Guadalajara, Mexico
Corazon Aquinodefeats incumbent Filipino dictatorFerdinand Marcosbut fraudulent returns gave the election to Marcos
Haiti's President-for-LifeJean-Claude Duvalierflees to France: Henri Namphy becomes leader of Haiti
Woody Allenfilm "Hannah and Her Sisters" starring Mia Farrow,Michael Caine,Dianne Wiestand an ensemble cast is released in the US
Madonna's "Open Your Heart" single goes #1
Tennis superstarBjörn Borgapparently attempts suicide in Milan, Italy
Bob Knight, Larry O'Brien, Tiny Archibald,Dave Cowens, Harry Gallatin & Larry Fleisher elected to NBA Hall of Fame
Provisional Irish Republican Army launches a mortar attack on 10 Downing Street during a cabinet meeting in an attempt to assassinate UK Prime MinisterJohn Major, injuring four people
Mike Tysontestifies in his rape trial
Carmen Electrareleases her self-titled debut album, "Carmen Electra"
Howard Sternstops a would-be jumper on the George Washington Bridge
Bluegrass and country musicianAlison Kraussreleases her compilation album "Now that I've Found You"
Mercury Nashville Records releases "The Woman in Me", the 2nd studio album byShania Twain(Billboard Album of the Year, 1996)
Uma Thurmanis named a knight of the Ordre des Arts et des Lettres of France for outstanding achievement in the field of art and literature
Super Bowl XLIV, Sun Life Stadium, Miami Gardens, Miami, FL; New Orleans Saints beat Indianapolis Colts, 31-17; MVP:Drew Brees, New Orleans, QB
Laureus World Sports Awards, Emirates Palace, Abu Dhabi, UAE: Sportsman:Rafael Nadal; Sportswoman:Lindsey Vonn; Team: Spanish Men's National Football team
Adam Ondracompletes the first ascent of La Dura Dura in Spain, his second 9b+ (5.15c) after Change
Angela Merkel's Christian Democratic Union party reaches a coalition deal with Social Democrats after four months of negotiations
Brock Purdysigns his letter of intent to commit to Iowa State University
Missing Nigerian painting masterpiece of Ife princess Adetutu Ademiluyi byBen Enwonwuannounced rediscovered in London
Super Bowl LV, Raymond James Stadium, Tampa, FL: Tampa Bay Buccaneers dominate defending champion Kansas City Chiefs, 31-9; MVP:Tom Brady, TB Bucs, QB; Brady's record 7th SB win
The Weekndbecomes the first Canadian solo artist to headline the Super Bowl half time show (Super Bowl LV)
French PresidentEmmanuel Macronmeets with Russian leaderVladimir Putinin Moscow amid build-up of Russian troops on border with Ukraine
LeBron Jamesscores 38 points to become NBA's all-time leading scorer as the Lakers go down 133-130 to Oklahoma City Thunder in Los Angeles; surpasses Kareem Abdul-Jabbar's 38,387 career points
US PresidentJoe Bidendelivers his state of the Union address, calling out the ultra-wealthy including Big Oil, addressing increasing threats from China and the killing of Tyre Nichols
Israeli Prime MinisterBenjamin Netanyahurejects Hamas's ceasefire proposal, saying he will continue till "total victory", saying forces will push further south into city of Rafah
Ukrainian President VolodymyrZelenskyyremoves his top general Valery Zaluzhny, in the military's most significant shake-up in years
Great Siege of Gibraltar, launched by France and Spain against the British colony during the American War of Independence is lifted after 3 years and 7 months
The Portland Vase, thought to date to the 1st century BC is shattered into more than 80 pieces by a drunken visitor to the British Museum
The Maastricht Treaty is signed by 12 countries from the European Community (EC) to create the European Union (EU)
Prince Shōtoku, Japanese regent and politician who promoted Buddhism (Hōryū-ji temple), born in Yamoto, Japan (d. 622)
Empress Matilda [Maud], Princess of England, claimant to the English throne as daughter of Henry I, wife of Holy Roman Emperor Henry V, born in Sutton Courtenay, England (d. 1169)
Thomas Killigrew, English dramatist and humorist (leader of the King's Men), born in London, England (d. 1683)
Jean-François Regnard, French comedy writer, dramatist and traveller (Slave in Algeria), born in Paris, France (d. 1709)
Anna Ivanovna, Empress of Russia (1730-40) [NS], born in Moscow (d. 1740)
Philippe Buache, French geographer and cartographer (pioneered use of contour lines), born in Paris, France (d. 1773)
Carl August Thielo, Danish composer, opera director, and court organist, born in Høsterkøb, Denmark (d. 1763)
Joseph Pouteau, French composer and organist, born in Chaumes-en-Brie, France (d. 1823)
Adam-Philippe Custine, French general (American Revolutionary War, French Army of the North, executed for treason during Reign of Terror), born in Metz, France (d. 1793)
Rhijnvis Feith, Dutch poet and writer (Thirsci), born in Zwolle (d. 1824)
Benedikt Schack, Bohemian-Austrian singer, composer, and friend of Wolfgang Mozart (originated role of Tamino in Die Zauberflöte), born in Mirotice, Bohemia (d. 1826)
John Rylands, English cloth merchant and philanthropist (Rylands & Sons, Manchester's 1st multi-millionaire), born in Parr, England (d. 1888)
Frederik Paludan-Muller, Danish Romantic poet (Danserinden), born in Kerteminde, Denmark (d. 1876)
Gardner Quincy Colton, American lecturer who was the first to use nitrous oxide as an anesthetic in dentistry, born in Georgia, Vermont (d. 1898)
LeRoy Pope Walker, American lawyer and 1st Confederate States Secretary of War (1861), born in Huntsville, Alabama (d. 1884)
Joaquin Gaztambide, Spanish composer (Una vieja), born in Tudela, Spain (d. 1870)
Richard Genee, Austrian playwright, librettist, and composer, born in Danzig, Kingdom of Prussia (d. 1895)
Cristóbal Oudrid, Spanish pianist, conductor, and orchestral and zarzuela composer (Rondeña; El molinero de Subiza), born in Badajoz, Spain (d. 1877)
Ricardo Palma, Peruvian writer and poet (Tradiciones Peruanas), born in Lima, Peru (d. 1919)
James Murray, Scottish lexicographer and philologist (Oxford English Dictionary), born in Denholm, Scotland (d. 1915)
Nicolaas Pierson, Dutch economist and politician (Prime Minister of the Netherlands 1897-1901), born in Amsterdam, Netherlands (d. 1909)
Alexandre Ribot, French statesman, Prime Minister of France (1892-3, 1895, 1914, 1917), born in Saint-Omer, France (d. 1923)
Ernst Franck, German composer and conductor, born in Munich, Kingdom of Bavaria (d. 1889)
Alfred Lyttelton, English politician and sportsman, 1st to represent England at cricket and football, born in London (d. 1913)
Bernard Maybeck, American architect (Palace of Fine Arts, San Francisco), born in New York City (d. 1957)
Mieczyslaw Soltys, Polish composer, born in Lemberg, Kingdom of Galicia. Austrian Empire (now Lviv, Ukraine) (d. 1929)
Arthur Collins, American baritone vocalist, known as 'King of the Ragtime Singers' (Peerless Quartet), and comedian (Collins & Harlan), born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 1933)
Ricardo Castro Herrera, Mexican concert pianist and composer, born at Hacienda de santa Bárbara, Mexico (d. 1907)
(Rafaël) "Raf" Verhulst, Flemish poet, journaliat, playwright (Jesus of Nazarus; Semini's Children), and educator, born in Wommelgem, Belgium (d. 1941)
Jindřich Šimon Baar, Czech pastor and writer (Jan Cimbura), born in Klenčí pod Čerchovem, Austria-HUngary (d. 1925)
(Carl) Wilhelm Stenhammar, Swedish composer considered the finest Swedish pianist of his time, born in Stockholm, Sweden (d. 1927)
Nikolaos Politis, Greek foreign minister and diplomat (Greek representative League of Nations), born in Corfu, Greece (d. 1943)
Erkki Melartin, Finnish composer (Juhlamarssi; Summer Symphony; Fantasia Apocaliptica), born in Käkisalmi, Grand Duchy of Finland (d. 1937)
Walter Courvoisier, Swiss composer, born in Riehen, Switzerland (d. 1931)
Pat Moran, American baseball catcher (World Series 1907 Chicago Cubs) and manager (World Series 1919 Cincinnati Reds), born in Fitchburg, Massachusetts (d. 1924)
Feliks Nowowiejski, Polish organist and composer, born in Wartenburg, East Prussia, German Empire (d. 1946)
G. H. Hardy, English mathematician (Hardy–Weinberg principle, A Mathematician's Apology), born in Cranleigh, England (d. 1947)
Julius Curtius, German Foreign Minister of the Weimar Republic (1929-31) and Minister for Economic Affairs (1926-29), born in Duisburg, Prussia (d. 1948)
Ossip Gabrilovich, Russian-American pianist, conductor (Detroit Symphony, 1918-36), and composer, born in Saint Petersburg, Russia (d. 1936)
Wilbur Sweatman, American ragtime and dixieland jazz clarinetist, composer, and bandleader, born in Brunswick, Missouri (d. 1961)[1]
[Harry] Sinclair Lewis, American writer and social critic (Nobel Prize in Literature 1930), born in Sauk Centre, Minnesota (d. 1951)
(James Hubert) "Eubie" Blake, American ragtime composer and pianist ("Memories of You"; "I'm Just Wild About Harry"), born in Baltimore, Maryland (d. 1983)[1]
Harry Nyquist, Swedish-American physicist and electronic engineer who contributed to communication theory (Nyquist–Shannon sampling theorem; Nyquist rate), born in Nilsby, Värmland, Sweden (d. 1976)
Ludwig Winder, Austrian-Czech writer and journalist, born in Šafov, Moravia, Austria-Hungary (d. 1946)
Ann Little [Mary Brooks], American actress, born in Mount Shasta, California (d. 1984)
Joachim Stutschewsky, Austrian-Israeli cellist, composer, and musicologist, born in Romni, Ukraine (d. 1982)
Nicanor Abelardo, Filipino classical music and kundiman song composer, born in San Miguel de Mayumo, Bulacan, Captaincy General of the Philippines (d. 1934)
Joe Devlin, American actor (Sam-Dick Tracy), born in Manhattan, New York (d. 1973)
Irving Aaronson, American jazz pianist and big band bandleader, born in New York City (d. 1963)
Harold Hoffman, American politician (Governor of New Jersey 1935-38), born in South Amboy, New Jersey (d. 1954)
Jacob Paludan, Danish writer and critic (Jorgen Stein), born in Copenhagen, Denmark (d. 1975)
Quincy Porter, American composer, teacher, and Pulitzer Prize winner (1954), born in New Haven, Connecticut (d. 1966)
Morgan Lee "Dock" Boggs, American folk and blues singer and banjo player, born in Norton, Virginia (d. 1971)
Arvid Pelshe, Latvian Communist leader (First Secretary of the Latvian SSR, CPSU Politburo member), born near Zālīte, Latvia (d. 1983)
Bob den Doolaard [Cornelis Spoelstra], Dutch writer and journalist (Grape Pickers), born in Zwolle, Netherlands (d. 1994)
Alison Leggatt, British character actress (Day of Triffids, Cows), born in Kensington, London (d. 1990)
Benode Behari Mukherjee, Indian blind artist (Lives of Medieval Saints fresco), born in Behala, British India (d. 1980)[1]
Milton Krims, American screenwriter (Prince of Foxes), born in New York City (d. 1988)
Paul Nizan, French journalist and writer (L'Humanité, La Conspiration), born in Tours, France (d. 1940)
Ulf von Euler, Swedish physiologist famous for his work on neurotransmitters (Nobel 1970), born in Stockholm, Sweden (d. 1983)
Oleg Antonov, Soviet aircraft designer, born in Troitsy, Russia(d. 1984)
Ray Colignon (sometimes none as "Coco"), Belgian jazz and easy listening pianist, Hammond organist, and bandleader, born in Liège, Belgium (d. 1987)
Yevgeniy Abalakov, Soviet mountaineer, first to climb Communism peak the highest point of the Soviet Union, born in Yeniseysk, Russia (d. 1948)
Clarence "Buster" Crabbe, American swimmer (Olympic gold 1932) and actor (Tarzan the Fearless, Flash Gordon), born in Oakland, California (d. 1983)
Hélder Câmara, Brazilian Catholic archbishop and human rights activist, Bishop of Brazil, born in Fortaleza, Brazil (d. 1999)
Alfred Desenclos, French composer, mostly of liturgical works (Messe de Requiem), born in Pontel, France (d. 1971)
Derek Farr, British actor (8 O'Clock Walk, Doctor at Large), born in London, England (d. 1986)
Russell Drysdale, Australian artist (painted Australian outback), born in Bognor Regis, England (d. 1981)
David Ignatow, American poet (Tread the Dark, Rescue the Dead), born in New York City (d. 1997)
Jacques Mornard [Ramon Mercader], Spanish communist (murdered Trotsky in Mexico with an ice axe), born in Argentona, Spain (d. 1978)
Ralph Whitlock, British farmer, conservationist, writer, and broadcaster, born in Pitton, Wiltshire,England (d. 1995)
Eddie Bracken, American actor (Summer Stock, Young & Willing), born in Astoria, New York (d. 2002)

Milt Holland [Milton Olshansky], American drummer, percussionist, ethno-musicologist, and tinkle-ist (Peter Pan film - Tinkerbell's tinkle; Bewitched - Samantha's nose tinkle), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 2005)
Markey Robinson, Irish painter, born in Belfast, Northern Ireland (d. 1999)
Ilse Pausin, Austrian figure skater (Olympic silver pairs 1936; World C'ship silver x 5 [with brother Erik Pausin]), born in Vienna, Austria (d. 1999)
Jock Mahoney [Jacques Joseph O'Mahoney], American actor and stuntman (Dallas, Yancy Derringer, Day of Fury), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 1989)
An Wang, Chinese-American computer engineer (Wang Labs; Wang Computers), born in Shanghai, China (d. 1990)[1]
Oscar Brand, Canadian-born American folk vocalist (Draw Me A Laugh), born in Winnipeg, Manitoba (d. 2016)
Athol Rowan,  South African cricket spin bowler (15 Tests, 54 wickets, BB 5/68; Transvaal), born in Johannesburg, South Africa (d. 1998)
Josephine "Hattie" Jacques, British comedy actress (Carry on films, Hancock's Half Hour), born in Kent, England (d. 1980)
George Lascelles, English Earl of Harewood, Director of the Royal Opera House, born in London, England (d. 2011)
Keefe Brasselle [John Brasselli], American actor (The Eddie Cantor Story, Be Our Guest), born in Elyria, Ohio (d. 1981)
Martha Holmes, American photographer and photojournalist, born in Louisville, Kentucky (d. 2006)
Cathy Small Long, American politician (Rep-D-LA, 1985-87), born in Dayton, Ohio (d. 2019)
Dora Bryan [Broadbent], British stage and screen actress (A Taste of Honey), novelty singer ("All I Want For Christmas Is A Beatle"), and hotelier, born in Southport England, (d. 2014)
Johnny Jordaan [Johannes van Musscher], Dutch pop and levenslied singer, born in Amsterdam, Netherlands (d. 1989)
Arthur Berry, English artist and playwright, born in Smallthorne, Stoke-on-Trent (d. 1994)
Herbert Eisenreich, Austrian writer, born in Linz (d. 1986)
Marius Constant, Romanian-born French composer and conductor (founded Ars Nova), born in Bucharest, Romania (d. 2004)
Romolo Valli, Italian actor (Bobby Deerfield, Fistful of Dynamite, La Viaccia), born in Reggio Emilia, Italy (d. 1980)
Konstantin Petrovich Feoktistov, Soviet cosmonaut (Voskhod 1 - 1st to carry three members), born in Voronezh, Russia, USSR (d. 2009)
Mark Taimanov, Russian pianist, born in Kharkiv, Ukraine (d. 2016)
John Buller, British contemporary classical composer (Proença,; Illusions), born in London, England (d. 2004)
Juliette Gréco, French singer (Sous le ciel de Paris) and actress, born in Montpellier, France (d. 2020)[1]
Laurence "Laurie" Johnson, British film and television score composer (Dr. Strangelove; The Avengers), born in Hampstead, England (d. 2024)[1]
Alphonse "Al" Smith [Fuzzy Smith], American MLB outfielder and third baseman (Cleveland Indians), born in Kirkwood, Missouri (d. 2002)
Gloria Talbott, American film and television actress, sometimes known as "the Scream Queen" for her work in B-movies, born in Glendale, California (d. 2000)
Alfred M. Worden, American Colonel USAF, NASA astronaut (Apollo 15), born in Jackson, Michigan (d. 2020)
